sutta: once the exalted 1 was dwelling near rajagaha at the squirrels feeding ground in the bamboo grove and there chundi the raja's daughter attended by 500 maidens in a light number of chariots came and visited him and after saluting sat down at 1 side thus seated chundi the raja's daughter spoke to the exalted 1 thus lord our royal brother chunda says this when a woman or man has taken refuge in the buddha has taken refuge in dhamma has taken refuge in the sangha abstains from taking life from taking what is not given from adultery from lying and from indulging in liquor causing idleness he will surely arise on the breaking up of the body after death to a fair cause not an evil 1 but i lord would ask the exalted 1 with 1's trust in what sort of teacher will 1 surely arise on the breaking up of the body after death to a fair cause and not to an ill 1 with 1's trust in what sort of dhamma what sort of sangha and what sort of virtuous practices must 1 perform to arise after death to a fair cause and not to an ill 1 whatsoever beings there are tundi whether footless 2-footed 4-footed or many-footed whether with bodies or without conscious or unconscious or neither conscious nor unconscious of them the tattagata the arahant the perfectly enlightened 1 is declared the best whosoever put the trust in the buddha put their trust in the best and unto them is the best reward whatsoever dharmas are formulated or not formulated chundi of them the dharma of dispassion is declared the best i mean the crushing of pride the quenching of thirst the rooting out of lust the cutting off of rebirth the destruction of craving dispassion making an end nibana whosoever put their trust in this dhamma put their trust in the best and unto them is the best reward whatsoever sangha or communities there are tunde of them the the sangha of the tatar gathers disciples is declared the best that is to say the 4 pairs of men the 8 persons that community of disciples of the exalted 1 which is worthy of offerings worthy of gifts worthy of oblations fit to be reverently saluted the world's fearless field for merit whosoever put their trust in this sangha put their trust in the best and unto them is the best reward whatsoever virtuous practices there are to indy of them those beloved loved by the aryans are declared the best that is to say those unbroken and without a rent and tarnished and without blemish bringing freedom praised by the wise incorrupt and conducive to concentration whosoever performed the virtues loved by the aryans performed the best and unto them is the best reward that's the end of the sutta
commentary: so here the buddha is saying that the best teacher to follow is the buddha himself not any other teacher it was decided at the second sangha council about 100 years after the buddha's passing away that if any monk teaches and we listen to him we should follow what he teaches only if what he teaches is in accordance with what the buddha taught in the dharma vinaya and you had heard earlier in 1 of the sutas in the book of the in the chapter of the force that the buddha said that we should compare whatever among teachers with the sutas and the vinaya so that is why we have to be very familiar with the dhamma the sutasa and the vinaya to be sure that whatever teacher teaches us is teaching us what the buddha himself says because our 1 and only teacher is the buddha other monks are what are called kalyana mitta good knowing friends who introduce us to the buddha's teachings and then in the mahaparinibana when the buddha was about to pass into nibana 1 of the monks asked him in future bhagawan when you are normal around who should we take as our teacher and the buddha said take the dhamma vinaya as your teacher so it is very clear that nowadays we must take the dhamma vinaya as our teacher now that the buddha has passed into nibana so that is why we stress the sutas as being the very foundation of the whole of the holy life and then of the dharmas the dharma of dispassion is declared the best the crushing of pride the quenching of thirst the rooting out of lust the cutting off of rebirth the destruction of craving making an end nibana this is the kind of dhamma we should pursue and we practice the holy path and is not actually concerned with getting this and getting that and some people are very concerned about getting uh this and getting that the paths and the fruitions and getting the knowledges etc but actually it is the cooling making an end that is important this is cutting off all our desires [Music] because 1 if you remember the 3 types of craving that bring us back to the round of rebirths 1 is craving for sensual pleasure second 1 is craving for existence and the third 1 is craving for non-existence this grieving for non-existence means that we pursue this idea of non-existence uh too much in the sense that the the the mind is too agitated about it and the mind is too agitated there is no cooling of the mind nibana is concerned with cooling cooling of the mind and letting go uh whatever thoughts etc we have to let go even this uh excessive desire for non-existence that has to be put down that is why it is a dispassion here the blessing is a quenching of this rooting out of lust etc and of all the sanghas the communities there are the buddha said the best 1 is his disciples these disciples are always called savakas in the bali and savakas means hearers the buddha always called his disciples hearers because they hear they listen to his and because of listening to his teachings they have unshakable faith in the buddha dharma and sangha that is why the buddha generally refers to his disciples as being aryan disciples once you have a very good foundation in the in the dhamma in the teachings of the buddha it is natural that the person from there becomes an arya and here the buddha says the 4 pairs of men the 8 persons the the the aryans are always 8 persons it is not 4 persons it's always 8 persons and that's about the commands i like to make and the last 1 is the precepts we have to keep the precepts and the virtues and that is the aryan virtues the precepts that are laid down by the buddha suta
